content:
11 min read
Just now

--

When a model has no real fact to give you, it usually doesn’t stay silent. It fills the gap with the most plausible continuation, and it sounds just as confident as when it’s right.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Ask a language model for a fact it doesn’t have, and watch what it does. It usually doesn’t stop. It gives you an answer, cleanly formatted, phrased with the same confidence it uses for things it actually knows. Ask it for a source to back a claim, and if it has no real one, it can build a citation from nothing: plausible authors, a plausible title, the right-looking journal and year, sometimes a DOI that resolves to nothing. The output is shaped exactly like a correct answer. That shape is the whole problem, because nothing on the surface separates the real answers from the invented ones.
In 2023 a lawyer in New York learned this in front of a federal judge. Steven Schwartz, representing a client in Mata v. Avianca, asked ChatGPT to find case law for his argument, and it returned six decisions with names, quotes, and internal citations. He filed them. None of the cases existed. When he grew nervous and asked ChatGPT whether they were real, it assured him they were. They still weren’t. The court sanctioned him, and the episode became the standard cautionary tale for what these models do when they run out of facts.
The courtroom story is dramatic, but the version that does the most damage in real systems is quieter. A model that gives a confident wrong answer is more dangerous than one that gives no answer, because a blank space gets noticed and a fluent paragraph does not. Picture an internal assistant built on a company’s documentation, or a support bot answering customers. Ask it something the documents cover and it answers well. Ask it something just outside that coverage, and a model that won’t say “I don’t know” will produce a clean, specific, wrong answer instead. The failure here isn’t loud. The system doesn’t crash; it just gives a wrong answer in a voice that sounds exactly like a right one. That answer passes review, reaches the user, and gets believed, because it reads like every correct answer next to it. If you read my earlier post on the Reversal Curse, this is the same kind of silent gap, approached from a different angle.
Hallucination, Confabulation, and Why the Word Matters
The field calls all of this “hallucination,” and the word has been stretched until it covers almost every way a model can be wrong. Sebastian Farquhar and colleagues, in a 2024 Nature paper, make the useful point that there’s no reason to expect a single mechanism behind every kind of error, so lumping them under one word hides more than it reveals. The standard surveys (Huang and colleagues, 2025) split the term along two axes. One is faithfulness: whether the output stays true to the source you gave the model. The other is factuality: whether the output is true about the world. A summary that contradicts the document it’s summarizing is a faithfulness failure. A confident claim about a person who doesn’t exist is a factuality failure. They look similar on the surface and come from different places.
This post is about one specific kind, and it has a better name: confabulation. The word comes from clinical psychology, where it describes a person who fills a gap in memory with a fabricated account, told sincerely and with full confidence, and with no intent to deceive. That last part matters. The person isn’t lying, because lying requires knowing the truth and choosing to hide it. They simply have a hole where a memory should be, and the mind produces something plausible to fill it. “Hallucination” implies perceiving something that isn’t there, which is the wrong picture for a language model. “Confabulation” implies filling a gap with a confident guess, which is exactly right. Farquhar’s group uses the term in a precise way, for the subset of errors that are arbitrary: answers that change if you run the model again with a different random seed, because there was never a stable fact behind them in the first place. I’ll use it the same way.
The intuition is the student who didn’t study but refuses to leave the answer blank. Asked a question they can’t recall, they write something that sounds like the textbook, in the right tone, with the right shape, and sometimes they’re even close. The model does this constantly, and it does it well, because producing text that sounds right is the one thing it was built to do.
Why It Happens
A language model is trained to predict the next token, the next chunk of text, given everything before it. That’s the whole objective. It does not have one mode for recalling a stored fact and another for making something up. It has a single mode: produce the most probable continuation of this text. When the fact you’re asking about appears often and consistently in the training data, the most probable continuation happens to be the true one, and the model looks like it’s remembering. When the fact is rare, or absent, or you’ve asked about something that was only ever stated once, the most probable continuation is still a fluent, well-formed answer. It just isn’t tied to anything true. The model has no way to feel the difference, because from the inside both cases are the same operation.
OpenAI’s 2025 paper “Why Language Models Hallucinate” makes this precise in two steps. The first is about pretraining. Even with perfectly clean training data, the authors argue, some rate of false statements is statistically unavoidable, because generating a valid answer is mathematically harder than judging whether an answer is valid, and the training objective is the harder of the two. When the model can’t reliably tell true from plausible-but-false, as with an obscure birthday it saw once or never, errors follow as a matter of statistics, not as a glitch. The second step is about why this survives all the training we do afterward. Most benchmarks score a wrong answer and an “I don’t know” the same way: both get zero. So a model optimized to do well on those benchmarks learns to behave like a student taking an exam with no penalty for guessing. Guessing always beats abstaining. Over millions of examples, the model learns to always produce an answer, because that is what gets rewarded. The authors’ proposed fix is not a cleverer model but a change in scoring: penalize confident wrong answers more than honest uncertainty, so that saying “I don’t know” stops being the losing move.
There’s a related reason these answers sound so convincing. Training to predict the next token tends to make a model well-calibrated, which means the probabilities it assigns line up, on average, with how often it’s right. Kadavath and colleagues showed in 2022 that models mostly carry an internal signal of their own confidence. But calibration is about being right on average across many answers. It says nothing about whether any single confident sentence is true. So the model can be calibrated and still hand you a specific, fluent, completely wrong statement, delivered in the same even tone as a correct one. That even tone is the dangerous part, and it’s a direct product of how the thing is trained.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What It Costs, and How to Build Around It
Start with the uncomfortable result: you cannot fully remove this. Two separate lines of work (Xu and colleagues, 2024; Banerjee and colleagues, 2024) argue that for any model of this kind, some hallucination is unavoidable in principle. So the engineering goal is never “eliminate it.” It’s reduce how often it happens and contain what gets through. Most of the real work lives in the system around the model, not in the model itself.
The single most effective move is to stop relying on the model’s memory for facts at all, and instead put the relevant facts in front of it. This is what retrieval-augmented generation, or RAG, does: before the model answers, a retrieval step finds relevant text from a trusted source and places it in the prompt, so the model can work from real material instead of recalling from its weights (Lewis and colleagues, 2020). When the answer is sitting in the context, the model tends to use it rather than invent one. But this is a reduction, not a cure, and it’s worth being honest about the gaps. If retrieval returns nothing relevant and the model still won’t abstain, it will confabulate over the empty context. It can also blend a real retrieved fact with an invented detail, or quietly contradict the source it was given. Grounding moves the problem, it doesn’t dissolve it.
The second move is to give the model a real way out, and reward it for taking it. In practice that means designing the prompt, the scoring, and any guardrails so that “I don’t know” or “the documents don’t cover this” is an acceptable, even preferred, answer, and setting a confidence threshold below which the system declines rather than guesses. There’s a genuine trade-off here: a model that abstains more often is less immediately useful, a tension OpenAI itself has flagged when noting that high refusal rates can frustrate users. You’re choosing where to sit on the line between answering too much and answering too confidently, and the right point depends on the cost of a wrong answer in your domain.
This connects to a shift that is only starting to take hold in the field, and it sits one level above any single system. The deeper fix the OpenAI paper argues for is about how models are scored and rewarded in the first place. As long as a benchmark gives a wrong answer and an honest “I don’t know” the same zero, models keep learning that a confident guess is the safe bet. Change the scoring so that honest uncertainty beats a confident error, and abstaining stops being the losing move during training, not only at inference time. That shift is recent and still partial, since most public leaderboards still reward guessing, but it is the most promising structural direction on the table, because it attacks the incentive that produces confabulation in the first place rather than cleaning up the output after the fact.
The third move is to verify before you trust. Where exact facts matter, check the model’s claims against the source: require citations that resolve to real documents, and reject the ones that don’t. You can also use the model’s own instability against it. Because confabulations are arbitrary, asking the same question several times and comparing the answers will often expose them: if the meaning is stable across samples, the model probably has something real; if the answers wander, treat it as a confabulation and don’t pass it on. This is the idea behind semantic entropy in the Nature paper, measuring disagreement at the level of meaning rather than wording. The cost is compute and latency, since you’re running the model more than once, so you reserve it for the answers where being wrong is expensive.
Underneath all of this is the cheap habit that matters most, and it’s the same one I argued for with the Reversal Curse: test for the failure on purpose. The confident wrong answer is invisible to any evaluation that only asks the model things it can answer. So build tests that ask for facts you know the system can’t ground, and watch whether it bluffs or admits it doesn’t know. If you only ever test the cases the model handles well, you’ll ship the confabulations and let your users find them.
My own view, from building these systems, is that the framing that keeps you safe is to treat the model as a reasoning and language layer over verified context, not as the source of truth itself. The model is very good at reading, rephrasing, summarizing, and reasoning over material you hand it. It is unreliable as a database you query from memory. Almost every robust design I’ve seen comes back to that split: keep the facts in a place you control, and let the model operate on them, rath
